figures.caption: Figure 1: Encoder-decoder architecture.

Figure 6: εL2 µ (G̃L2 µ−RB-NO) for the diffusion problem (27) over network depth and width for different input smoothness s. Top row: GELU activation function; bottom row: tanh activation function. Coefficient-space dimensions fixed at din = dout = 200, training set size n = 104.

Figure 7: εL2 µ (G̃H1 µ−RB-NO) for the diffusion problem (27) over network depth and width for different input smoothness s. Coefficient-space dimensions fixed at din = dout = 200, training set size n = 104.
Figure 2: Input samples constructed as in (26) using a fixed coefficient vector c and varying s values. As s decreases, the correlation length becomes smaller.
Figure 3: A random parameter field x(ξ) (left) and corresponding FE solution y(ξ) of the linear elliptic PDE (27) (right).
Figure 14: εL2 µ (G̃RB-TT) for the elliptic PDE (27) over number of PDE solves n and TT surrogate size N for din = 100, θ = 1.0 and s = 2.0. ALSx performs x end-to-end iterations over the TT.
Figure 13: εL2 µ (G̃RB-TT) for the elliptic PDE (27) over number of PDE solves n and TT surrogate size N for din = 100 and s = 2.0.
Figure 12: εL2 µ of G̃L2 µ−RB-NO and G̃sL2 µ−RB-NO as in (32) in the diffusion problem (27). Fixed din = dout = 200, s = 2 and network depth 9 and width 100.

Figure 11: εL2 µ (G̃) in the diffusion problem (27) using H1(Ω)-orthogonal or L2(Ω)-orthogonal basis functions to construct the decoder D. Fixed din = dout = 200 and s = 2. G̃RB-SG is constructed using hyperparameters a = b = 1 and G̃L2 µ−RB-NO using depth 9 and width 100.
Figure 16: εL2 µ -convergence over number of training data n for different s.
Figure 17: εH1 µ -convergence over number of training data points n for different s.

Figure 18: εL2 µ -error versus surrogate evaluation time tE per sample for the diffusion problem (27). The top row shows results obtained when evaluating a single sample, while the bottom row shows evaluation time per sample when evaluating the surrogates on a batch of size nbatch = 104, for different s.
Figure 4: 2D deformation problem
Figure 5: A random parameter field x(ξ) (left) and corresponding FE solution y(ξ) of the hyperelasticity problem (right).

Figure 10: εL2 µ for the diffusion problem (27) in dependence of encoder and decoder dimensions, din and dout, for various values of s and n = 104 training samples. G̃L2 µ−RB-NO uses networks of depth 7 (for s ∈ {0.5, 1.0}) or depth 9 (for s ∈ {2.0, 3.0}) and width 200, while G̃H1 µ−RB-NO uses networks of depth 7 and width 400. G̃RB-SG is constructed using hyperparameters a = 0.5 and b = 1.2. These hyperparameters were selected to lie within regimes of low surrogate error as shown in Figures 6, 7 and 9.
Figure 19: Evaluation cost over batch size for s = 2 and εL2 µ ∼ 10−3 for the diffusion problem (27).
Figure 20: εL2 µ -convergence over parametric surrogate complexity N for different s for the diffusion problem (27).
Table 1: Architectural parameters used throughout the experiments in Section 6.3.
Figure 8: εL2 µ (G̃FNO) for the diffusion problem (27) over the number of channels and Fourier modes for different input smoothness s. Network depth L = 4, training set size n = 104.

Figure 9: εL2 µ (G̃RB-SG) for the diffusion problem (27) in dependence of the interpolation hyperparameters a and b as in (20) for different operator input smoothness s, with the minimum of the error landscape marked by ⋆. Fixed din = dout = 200 and n = 104.
Figure 21: εL2 µ -convergence over training/setup time tT for different s for the diffusion problem (27).
Figure 15: εL2 µ (G̃RB-TT) for the elliptic PDE (27) over TT surrogate size N for din = 100 and s = 2.0.
